original_hypothesis.title: Missing Access Control on upgradeTo/upgradeToAndCall Allows Unauthorized Upgrades
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: WellUpgradeable.upgradeTo() and upgradeToAndCall() override OpenZeppelin's UUPS functions but do not include `onlyOwner` or any msg.sender check. The _authorizeUpgrade function only validates the new implementation against the Aquifer registry, not the caller's identity. If the _authorizeUpgrade checks were functional (currently broken per TECH-H003), any address could upgrade any WellUpgradeable proxy to any Aquifer-registered implementation.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Standard UUPS pattern requires _authorizeUpgrade to check msg.sender. OwnableUpgradeable is inherited and initialized in init(), providing an owner role, but it's never used in the upgrade path. This is a missing access control code defect. While currently mitigated by TECH-H003 (broken auth checks), if that bug is fixed, this becomes immediately exploitable.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No onlyOwner modifier on upgradeTo/upgradeToAndCall; _authorizeUpgrade has no msg.sender check; OwnableUpgradeable inherited but onlyOwner never applied to upgrade path

preconditions: _authorizeUpgrade checks must pass (currently broken, requiring fix of TECH-H003)
Attacker needs a valid well implementation registered in the same Aquifer
Aquifer boreWell is permissionless (attacker can register malicious implementations)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Missing onlyOwner on upgradeTo/upgradeToAndCall; _authorizeUpgrade validates implementation but not caller
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Any address can upgrade the well to any Aquifer-registered implementation, including malicious ones with drain functions
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No msg.sender == owner() check anywhere in the upgrade path

analysis_questions: Is Aquifer.boreWell permissionless? Can anyone register an implementation?
Could fixing TECH-H003 (wrong mapping query) inadvertently expose this vulnerability?
Are there any other guards (e.g., in OZ internals) that prevent unauthorized upgrades?
validation_requirements: Verify no msg.sender check in entire upgrade code path
Simulate upgrade from non-owner address (assuming auth checks pass)
Verify OZ UUPSUpgradeable base doesn't add implicit sender checks

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: If auth checks were functional: Attacker deploys malicious well implementation via Aquifer (permissionless boreWell). Attacker calls upgradeTo(maliciousImpl) on victim well. Auth passes (valid aquifer-registered impl). Well logic changes to attacker-controlled code. Attacker drains all pool tokens.
prerequisites: TECH-H003 must be fixed first (or ERC1967 slot properly initialized)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete loss of all assets in the well

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying that non-owner cannot call upgradeTo

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Minimal - just gas for bore + upgrade txs

validation_result.mechanism: missing access control on UUPS upgrade path
validation_result.impact_summary: upgradeTo() and upgradeToAndCall() lack any msg.sender authorization check, allowing any address to upgrade the proxy implementation if the Aquifer-based validation passes. Currently mitigated by a secondary bug (TECH-H003) that bricks upgrades entirely.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: WellUpgradeable inherits OwnableUpgradeable and initializes ownership in init(), but the onlyOwner modifier is never applied to upgradeTo(), upgradeToAndCall(), or _authorizeUpgrade(). The _authorizeUpgrade function validates: (1) execution is via delegatecall, (2) current implementation is registered in Aquifer, (3) new implementation is registered in Aquifer, and (4) new implementation supports ERC-1967. It never checks msg.sender == owner(). The standard UUPS pattern requires _authorizeUpgrade to enforce caller identity. OpenZeppelin's default UUPSUpgradeable.upgradeTo has an onlyProxy modifier and delegates access control to _authorizeUpgrade, which projects must implement with ownership checks. WellUpgradeable overrides upgradeTo entirely, removing the onlyProxy modifier and replacing it with custom logic that omits sender verification. This is a textbook missing access control defect (SWC-118).
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 62-83 of WellUpgradeable.sol confirm: upgradeTo() at line 76 and upgradeToAndCall() at line 83 have no onlyOwner modifier and no msg.sender check. _authorizeUpgrade() at lines 62-74 performs four require checks, none of which involve msg.sender or owner(). OwnableUpgradeable is inherited (line 16) and initialized in init() (line 42: __Ownable_init()), but onlyOwner is never referenced in the upgrade path.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No onlyOwner modifier or msg.sender check. Any address can call this function. The only gate is _authorizeUpgrade which validates the implementation addresses but not the caller.
Same as upgradeTo - no access control on who can call. Any address can trigger an upgrade with arbitrary calldata.

Four require checks validate implementation addresses and delegatecall context, but none check msg.sender. The standard UUPS pattern mandates that _authorizeUpgrade include caller authorization (e.g., onlyOwner).

calling_sequence: 1. Any address calls upgradeTo(newImplementation) on the proxy
3. _authorizeUpgrade checks: address(this) != ___self (delegatecall check passes on proxy)
7. NO check on msg.sender anywhere in the path
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The entire upgrade path from upgradeTo/upgradeToAndCall through _authorizeUpgrade lacks any msg.sender verification. OwnableUpgradeable is inherited and initialized at WellUpgradeable.sol:42 (__Ownable_init()), but the onlyOwner modifier is never used on any upgrade function. If TECH-H003 were fixed (correct Aquifer mapping query), any address could upgrade the proxy to any Aquifer-registered implementation.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment scripts show encodeAndBoreWellUpgradeable in WellDeployer.sol but no special access control setup beyond init(). No deployment scripts specifically deploy WellUpgradeable instances.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack requires TECH-H003 to be fixed first. If fixed: (1) Attacker deploys malicious WellUpgradeable implementation. (2) Attacker registers it via Aquifer.boreWell (permissionless). (3) Attacker calls upgradeTo(maliciousImpl) on victim proxy. (4) _authorizeUpgrade passes all checks since malicious impl is Aquifer-registered. (5) Proxy implementation is changed to attacker-controlled code. (6) Attacker calls drain function on the now-malicious proxy. Currently, TECH-H003 blocks this by causing _authorizeUpgrade to revert (wrong mapping query), but the missing access control defect is real.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL code defect. If TECH-H003 is independently fixed without also adding onlyOwner, any address can upgrade any WellUpgradeable proxy to any Aquifer-registered implementation. This would enable complete loss of all pool assets. The defect exists in the code regardless of current exploitability - the access control is simply missing from a function that requires it per the UUPS pattern specification.
recommended_mitigations: Add onlyOwner modifier to both upgradeTo() and upgradeToAndCall() functions
Alternatively, add require(msg.sender == owner(), "Only owner can upgrade") to _authorizeUpgrade()
Add comprehensive tests verifying non-owner addresses cannot call upgrade functions
Consider adding a timelock to upgrade operations for additional safety
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Aquifer, WellUpgradeable implementation, and bore a well proxy. 2. Call init() on proxy to set owner. 3. Deploy second WellUpgradeable implementation and bore it via same Aquifer (to register it). 4. Fix TECH-H003 by initializing ERC1967 slot or adjusting _authorizeUpgrade logic. 5. From a non-owner address, call upgradeTo(secondImpl) on the proxy. 6. Verify the upgrade succeeds without owner authorization.
uncertainties: TECH-H003 currently prevents exploitation by bricking the upgrade path entirely - the interaction between these two bugs creates an accidental safeguard
If Aquifer.boreWell has any access restrictions not visible in scope, it could limit the attacker's ability to register malicious implementations
The exact behavior of IAquifer.wellImplementation is not in scope - only the interface is used
additional_testing_needed: Verify Aquifer.boreWell is permissionless (allows anyone to register implementations)
Test that fixing TECH-H003 alone (without adding onlyOwner) exposes this vulnerability
Test upgrade from non-owner address with mocked Aquifer that returns correct values
Verify OpenZeppelin UUPSUpgradeable internals don't add implicit sender checks in _upgradeToAndCallUUPS
